function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
If Starlink satellite useful lives proved shorter than assumed, which financial statement line items would be affected first and how would the impact differ between operating income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA?

steps.reasoning_content:
This question asks about the accounting impact if Starlink satellite useful lives proved shorter than assumed. I need to:

1. First, understand how SpaceX accounts for Starlink satellites - what depreciation method, useful life assumptions, and which segment they belong to.
2. Determine which financial statement line items would be affected.
3. Understand the difference between operating income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA for the Starlink segment.

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieving relevant information about satellite accounting policies, depreciation, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA definitions.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about satellite useful lives, depreciation policies, and Segment Adjusted EBITDA definitions.

arguments.prompt: What are the useful life assumptions and depreciation methods for Starlink satellites in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is the definition of Segment Adjusted EBITDA and what adjustments are made to reconcile it from operating income in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is the accounting policy for property and equipment depreciation including satellite depreciation in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the Significant Accounting Policies and related notes, here are the useful life assumptions, depreciation methods, and capitalization policies for Starlink satellites and related assets:

### 1. Depreciation Methods
*   **Straight-Line Method:** Satellites (along with most other Property, Plant, and Equipment) are depreciated using the **straight-line method** over their estimated useful lives. *(Note: This differs from reusable flight vehicles, which are depreciated based on the expected number of average flights).*
*   **Capitalized Additions:** Capitalized interest and significant enhancements/refurbishments are also depreciated ratably (straight-line) over the asset's identified useful life.

### 2. Useful Life Assumptions & Estimates
*   **Estimation Criteria:** Management estimates satellite useful lives based on a combination of engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology.
*   **Specific Lifespan Estimates:**
    *   **Broadband Satellites:** Estimated useful life of **5 years**.
    *   **First-Generation Mobile Satellites:** Estimated useful life of **3 years**.
    *   **General PP&E Table Classification:** Satellites are broadly categorized with an estimated useful life of **3 to 5 years**.
*   **Periodic Reviews & Adjustments:** Management periodically reviews these estimates with engineering and operations teams. If new data indicates that the useful lives are shorter or longer than originally estimated, the rate of depreciation is adjusted to reflect the revised timeline.

### 3. Early Retirements and Proactive Deorbiting
*   **Safety-Driven Deorbiting:** The Company frequently deorbits satellites **before** the end of their estimated useful lives. This is primarily done to reduce degradation risks that could impair the autonomous collision avoidance system and compromise overall constellation safety.
*   **AI Compute Hardware Evolution:** For orbital AI compute satellites, retirements may also occur sooner than estimated. As more advanced AI hardware becomes available, older hardware is shifted to lower-intensity workloads and eventually retired via controlled end-of-life disposition (e.g., graveyard orbits) to maintain optimal compute economics.

### 4. Capitalization Policies (Build, Launch, Maintenance & Refurbishment)
*   **Base Satellite Costs:** The capitalized cost of a satellite includes the costs to build it (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as **capitalized launch costs** incurred by the Space segment to deliver the satellite to orbit (including an allocation of flight vehicle costs).
*   **Interest Capitalization:** Qualified interest costs incurred during the significant acquisition or construction phase (before the asset is ready for its intended use) are capitalized as part of the asset's historical cost and depreciated over its useful life.
*   **Maintenance vs. Refurbishment:**
    *   **Expensed:** Routine expenditures for maintenance and repairs that *do not* extend the life of the asset are expensed as incurred.
    *   **Capitalized:** Significant refurbishments, renewals, and enhancements that increase the functionality, output, or expected life of an asset are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.

Based on the provided text, **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding certain items. 

To reconcile Segment Adjusted EBITDA from segment income (loss) from operations, the following adjustments are made (excluded/added back):
1. Depreciation and amortization
2. Share-based compensation
3. Restructuring charges
4. Impairment

Based on the provided financial statement excerpts, here is the comprehensive accounting policy for Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E) depreciation, with a specific focus on satellites and aerospace assets:

### 1. General Depreciation Methodology
*   **Valuation:** PP&E is stated at historical cost less accumulated depreciation.
*   **Primary Method:** The Company depreciates its assets primarily using the **straight-line method** over their estimated useful lives.
*   **Leasehold Improvements:** Depreciated over the **shorter** of their estimated useful lives or the related lease term.
*   **Capitalized Interest:** Interest costs incurred during the construction of significant long-term projects (e.g., AI data centers, launch facilities) are capitalized as part of the asset's historical cost and depreciated over the asset's estimated useful life once placed in service.

### 2. Satellite Depreciation & Capitalization Policy
*   **Depreciable Base (Cost):** Satellite costs include the costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as **capitalized launch costs** incurred by the Space segment to launch them into orbit (which includes an allocation of the flight vehicle's costs).
*   **Estimated Useful Lives:** 
    *   **Broadband satellites:** 5 years
    *   **First-generation mobile satellites:** 3 years
    *   *(General classification table lists Satellites as 3 - 5 years)*
*   **Factors Considered for Useful Life Estimation:** Management estimates satellite useful lives based on engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, orbit-raise timing, expected service capability, design enhancements across generations, the evolution of constellation density and technology, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology.
*   **Sensitivity:** A change in the average useful life of satellite assets by just one year would have a highly material impact on operating income (e.g., an estimated ~$480 million impact for the full year 2025).

### 3. Flight Vehicle & Spacecraft Depreciation (Exception to Straight-Line)
*   **Depreciation Method:** Unlike other PP&E, reusable flight vehicles and spacecraft (e.g., boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, Dragon capsules) are **not** depreciated using the straight-line method. Instead, they are depreciated based on the **expected number of average flights** for each vehicle.
*   **Estimated Useful Life:** 5 to 25 flights.
*   **Factors Considered for Flight Estimation:** 
    1. Continued ability to successfully recover and refurbish the hardware.
    2. Economic feasibility of incremental flights (supported by declining refurbishment costs).
    3. Customer acceptance limits for reflown hardware.
    4. Expected future launches in the mission manifest.
    5. Anticipated retirement timing of older models (e.g., Falcon) due to transitions to new vehicles (e.g., Starship).

### 4. Estimated Useful Lives of Other PP&E
| Classification | Estimated Useful Life |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Servers and networking equipment** | 5 - 6 years |
| **Machinery and equipment** | 3 - 10 years |
| **Data center infrastructure** | 20 - 25 years |
| **Launch sites** | 7 - 20 years |
| **Buildings and improvements** | 30 years |
| **Leasehold improvements** | Shorter of 7 - 20 years or the life of the lease |

### 5. Maintenance, Refurbishment, and Enhancements
*   **Expensed:** Routine expenditures for maintenance and repairs that do not extend the lives of the assets are expensed as incurred. Routine asset disposals, scrapping, and gateway decommissions are charged to Cost of Revenue or SG&A.
*   **Capitalized:** Significant refurbishments, renewals, and enhancements that increase the functionality, output, or expected life of an asset are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.

### 6. Changes in Accounting Estimates & Impairment
*   **Revisions to Useful Lives:** Management periodically reviews useful life and average flight estimates with engineering and operations teams. If it is determined that the useful lives or expected remaining flights are shorter or longer than originally estimated, the Company **adjusts the rate of depreciation** prospectively to reflect the revised estimates.
*   **Impairment Evaluation:** PP&E is evaluated for impairment at the lowest level of identifiable, independent cash flows whenever events or circumstances indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable (e.g., technological obsolescence, damaged flight vehicles, abandoned production lines). If future cash flows are less than the carrying value, an impairment charge is recognized to write the asset down to its estimated fair value.

steps.reasoning_content: I now have very good information. Let me also retrieve the specific Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table to see the exact line items and how depreciation is treated.

arguments.prompt: Show the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table with specific dollar amounts for Starlink segment in {{spacex_s1}}
What is the sensitivity analysis for satellite useful life changes and the estimated impact on operating income in {{spacex_s1}}?
What are the Starlink segment depreciation and amortization amounts in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial documents, Starlink's financial results are reported under the **Connectivity segment** (the text explicitly notes: *"Our Connectivity segment, primarily driven by Starlink..."*). 

Below is the consolidated **Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table** for the Connectivity (Starlink) segment, detailing the specific dollar amounts (in millions) across the reported periods:

### **Connectivity (Starlink) Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation**
*(in millions)*

| Line Item | Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 | Three Months Ended March 31, 2025 | Year Ended December 31, 2025 | Year Ended December 31, 2024 | Year Ended December 31, 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income from operations** | **$1,188** | **$1,033** | **$4,423** | **$2,006** | **$469** |
| *Add:* | | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 783 | 510 | 2,376 | 1,508 | 884 |
| Share-based compensation | 116 | 75 | 369 | 296 | 249 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | — | — | — |
| Impairment | — | — | — | 39 | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$2,087** | **$1,618** | **$7,168** | **$3,849** | **$1,602** |

### **Key Takeaways from the Reconciliation:**
* **Consistent Profitability & Growth:** Starlink (Connectivity) has shown massive sequential and year-over-year growth in Adjusted EBITDA, scaling from **$1,602 million in 2023** to **$7,168 million in 2025**, and reaching **$2,087 million in Q1 2026** alone.
* **Primary Adjustments:** The bridge between GAAP Income from Operations and Non-GAAP Adjusted EBITDA is predominantly driven by **Depreciation and Amortization** (reflecting the heavy capital expenditures and depreciation of capitalized satellite/launch costs as the LEO constellation scales) and **Share-based compensation**.
* **Impairment:** A minor impairment charge of **$39 million** was recorded in the 2024 fiscal year, but no restructuring or impairment charges have impacted the segment's EBITDA in the other reported periods.

Based on the provided text, the sensitivity analysis for changes in the estimated useful life of satellite assets is as follows:

If the average useful life of the satellite assets were changed by **one year**, the estimated impact on operating income would be:
* **For the year ended December 31, 2025:** Approximately **$480 million**
* **For the three months ended March 31, 2026:** Approximately **$170 million**

Based on the provided text, the **total, absolute depreciation and amortization amounts** for the Starlink (Connectivity) segment are **not explicitly disclosed**. 

However, the text does detail the **increases in depreciation** and how it impacted the segment's Cost of Revenue across different periods. Here are the specific depreciation-related figures and drivers mentioned for the Starlink/Connectivity segment:

*   **Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025:** The Connectivity segment's Cost of Revenue increased by $437 million. This was primarily driven by an **increase in depreciation of $276 million** from capitalized launch and satellite costs, which was due to the increased number of satellites placed into orbit and Starlink flights.
*   **Full Year 2025 vs. 2024:** The Connectivity segment's Cost of Revenue increased by $1,153 million, which was partially driven by **"higher depreciation as the number of satellites placed into orbit grew."** (No specific dollar amount is isolated for this increase).
*   **Full Year 2024 vs. 2023:** The Connectivity segment's Cost of Revenue increased by $1,982 million, partially due to **"higher depreciation of launch costs driven by an increase in the number of satellites placed into orbit."** (No specific dollar amount is isolated for this increase).

*Note: The text mentions that "Segment Adjusted EBITDA" excludes depreciation and amortization, but it does not provide the financial reconciliation tables required to back into the total D&A expense for the Connectivity segment.*

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check if there's any information about where satellite depreciation is classified in the income statement (cost of revenue vs. operating expenses).

arguments.prompt: Where is satellite depreciation expense classified in the income statement - cost of revenue or operating expenses in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is the impairment policy for satellites and how are early satellite retirements or deorbiting accounted for in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial text, satellite depreciation expense is classified in the **Cost of revenue** (not as a general operating expense like R&D or SG&A). 

This is explicitly supported by the following excerpts from the text:
* **Segment Accounting Policies:** *"Connectivity segment’s cost of revenue includes depreciation (inclusive of launch, **satellite**, and ground infrastructure costs)..."*
* **MD&A Cost Drivers:** When explaining the year-over-year increases in the **Cost of revenue**, the text repeatedly cites satellite depreciation as a primary driver. For example: *"This increase was primarily due to higher depreciation of $827 million from capitalized launch and **satellite costs**..."* and *"higher depreciation of $276 million from capitalized launch and **satellite costs**..."* 

*(Note: While the company does record some depreciation in R&D and SG&A for specific equipment and facilities, the depreciation of the actual satellites placed into orbit is strictly routed through Cost of revenue).*

Based on the provided financial and operational disclosures, here is the company's impairment policy for satellites and how early retirements or deorbiting are accounted for:

### **Impairment Policy for Satellites**
Satellites are classified as Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E) and are subject to the company’s overarching PP&E impairment evaluation process:
*   **Evaluation Level:** Impairment is assessed at the lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent of other assets and liabilities.
*   **Triggering Events:** The company reviews satellites for impairment whenever events or circumstances indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable. Indicators include technological developments that reduce the utility of existing assets, significant changes in use, declines in forecasted cash flows, negative industry trends, or physical damage (e.g., from launch anomalies, space weather, or debris).
*   **Measurement:** If estimated future cash flows are less than the asset's carrying value, an impairment charge is recognized to the extent the carrying value exceeds its estimated fair value.
*   **Income Statement Classification:** 
    *   **Routine/Recurring:** Routine asset disposals, scrapping, and recurring operational losses are charged to **Cost of revenue** or **Selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses**, depending on the asset's nature.
    *   **Non-Routine:** If the impairment is considered outside the normal course of business (e.g., write-offs of damaged satellite hardware or abandoned production lines), it is charged to the **Impairment** line on the consolidated statements of operations.
*   **Lack of Insurance:** The company generally does not insure its in-orbit satellites. Therefore, it bears the full financial cost of any satellite losses, making these impairment and depreciation adjustments direct hits to the company's financial condition.

### **Accounting for Early Satellite Retirements and Deorbiting**
The company estimates the useful life of its broadband satellites at 5 years and first-generation mobile satellites at 3 years. However, satellites are frequently retired or deorbited *before* the end of these estimated useful lives. 

**Reasons for Early Deorbiting/Retirement:**
*   **Proactive Safety & Collision Avoidance:** The company often deorbits satellites early to reduce degradation risks that could impair autonomous collision avoidance systems and compromise constellation safety.
*   **Orbital AI Compute Lifecycle:** For orbital AI infrastructure, older hardware may be retired sooner than estimated via controlled end-of-life disposition (e.g., transitioning to graveyard orbits) as performance characteristics evolve and newer AI hardware becomes available.
*   **Anomalies & Damage:** Incorrect orbital placement, space weather (geomagnetic storms/radiation), or component failures can render a satellite inoperable or truncate its lifespan.

**How Early Retirements are Accounted For:**
1.  **Accelerated Depreciation (Useful Life Adjustments):** When management and engineering teams determine that the useful lives of satellite assets are shorter than originally estimated (due to early deorbiting plans, propellant life, or planned transitions to newer technology), the company **adjusts the rate of depreciation** to reflect the revised, shorter useful life. 
2.  **Impairment Charges / Write-offs:** If early retirement is driven by sudden inoperability, damage, or anomalies that are deemed outside the normal course of business, the remaining carrying value of the satellite is written off as an **impairment charge**.
3.  **Routine Operational Expensing:** If the early deorbiting or scrapping is deemed a routine, recurring operational event (normal course of business), the remaining net book value or associated losses are expensed to **Cost of revenue** or **SG&A**.

### **Financial Sensitivity and Impact**
Because the company proactively manages its constellation density and frequently deorbits assets early, the estimated useful life of satellites is a critical accounting estimate. The company disclosed that if the average useful life of its satellite assets were changed by just **one year**, it would impact operating income by approximately **$480 million** for a full fiscal year (and $170 million for a single quarter).

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed to provide a thorough answer. Let me compile the analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me synthesize:

1. **Which financial statement line items would be affected first:**
   - Satellite depreciation is classified in **Cost of Revenue** for the Connectivity (Starlink) segment
   - If useful lives are shorter, depreciation expense increases → Cost of Revenue increases
   - This flows through to reduce **Gross Profit** and then **Operating Income**
   - If the shortening is sudden/non-routine, it could also trigger **Impairment charges**

2. **How the impact differs between Operating Income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
   - Operating Income: Directly reduced by higher depreciation (since depreciation is in Cost of Revenue)
   - Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Depreciation is **added back** to Operating Income to arrive at Segment Adjusted EBITDA, so the higher depreciation would be excluded from Segment Adjusted EBITDA
   - However, if the shorter useful life triggers an impairment charge, that impairment is also added back to Segment Adjusted EBITDA
   - So Segment Adjusted EBITDA would be largely insulated from the depreciation increase, while Operating Income would take the full hit

The sensitivity disclosure says a one-year change in satellite useful life would impact operating income by ~$480 million for FY2025 and ~$170 million for Q1 2026.

Let me also note the Connectivity segment reconciliation:
- FY2025: Income from operations $4,423M + D&A $2,376M + SBC $369M + Impairment $0 = Segment Adjusted EBITDA $7,168M
- The D&A add-back is the single largest reconciliation item

This is a great question because it highlights the divergence between GAAP operating income and non-GAAP Segment Adjusted EBITDA when it comes to capital-intensive businesses with significant depreciation.

arguments.final_result:
## Impact of Shorter Starlink Satellite Useful Lives on Financial Statement Line Items

### 1. First-Affected Line Items

If Starlink satellite useful lives proved shorter than assumed, the following line items would be affected **first and most directly**:

| Order | Line Item | Direction | Mechanism |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **1st** | **Depreciation Expense** (within Cost of Revenue) | ↑ Increase | Shorter useful life → higher periodic straight-line depreciation charge per satellite |
| **2nd** | **Cost of Revenue** (Connectivity segment) | ↑ Increase | Satellite depreciation is explicitly classified within the Connectivity segment's Cost of Revenue (inclusive of launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure depreciation) |
| **3rd** | **Gross Profit** | ↓ Decrease | Higher Cost of Revenue with no change in revenue |
| **4th** | **Income from Operations (Operating Income)** | ↓ Decrease | Flows directly from reduced Gross Profit |
| **5th** | **Net PP&E** (Balance Sheet) | ↓ Decrease | Accelerated accumulated depreciation reduces net book value of satellite assets |

Additionally, if the shorter useful life determination is triggered by a **sudden or non-routine event** (e.g., systemic component failure, space weather damage, or technological obsolescence), an **Impairment charge** could also be recognized as a separate line item on the income statement, further reducing Operating Income.

### 2. Divergence Between Operating Income and Segment Adjusted EBITDA

This is where the critical analytical distinction emerges. The Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation is:

| Reconciliation Item | FY 2025 ($M) | Q1 2026 ($M) |
|---|---|---|
| Income from Operations | $4,423 | $1,188 |
| **+ Depreciation & Amortization** | **$2,376** | **$783** |
| + Share-Based Compensation | $369 | $116 |
| + Restructuring Charges | $— | $— |
| + Impairment | $— | $— |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** | **$2,087** |

**Operating Income Impact:**
- The full incremental depreciation expense from shortened useful lives would reduce Operating Income dollar-for-dollar.
- SpaceX disclosed a **sensitivity estimate**: a one-year change in average satellite useful life would impact Operating Income by approximately **$480 million** for a full fiscal year (and **$170 million** for a single quarter, based on Q1 2026).
- If an impairment charge were also triggered, Operating Income would be further reduced by the write-down amount.

**Segment Adjusted EBITDA Impact:**
- **Depreciation and amortization is fully added back** in the reconciliation from Operating Income to Segment Adjusted EBITDA. Therefore, the incremental depreciation from shortened useful lives would be **completely excluded** from Segment Adjusted EBITDA.
- **Impairment charges are also added back** in the reconciliation. So even if a non-routine impairment were recorded, it too would be excluded from Segment Adjusted EBITDA.
- **Net result: Segment Adjusted EBITDA would be largely or entirely insulated** from the accounting impact of shorter satellite useful lives.

### 3. Quantifying the Divergence

Using the disclosed sensitivity:

| Metric | Impact of 1-Year Shorter Useful Life (FY 2025 basis) |
|---|---|
| Operating Income | **↓ ~$480 million** |
| Segment Adjusted EBITDA | **~$0 impact** (depreciation add-back neutralizes) |
| Divergence (Operating Income vs. Adj. EBITDA) | **~$480 million** |

### 4. Analytical Implications

This creates a **material divergence** between the two profitability measures that investors should understand:

- **Operating Income** reflects the economic reality that satellites are being consumed faster than planned — the capital deployed per year of actual service is higher than originally modeled.
- **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**, by excluding all depreciation, **masks this capital consumption acceleration entirely**. A scenario in which satellites last only 3 years instead of 5 would require significantly higher ongoing capital expenditure to maintain constellation density, but this cash outflow would appear only in CapEx (investing activities), not in Adjusted EBITDA.
- The filing itself flags this as a **critical accounting estimate**, noting that management periodically reviews useful life assumptions with engineering teams based on on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions.
- The company also acknowledges that it **frequently deorbits satellites before the end of their estimated useful lives** for safety and collision-avoidance reasons, making this a live risk rather than a hypothetical one.

### 5. Summary

| | Operating Income | Segment Adjusted EBITDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Impact** | ↓ Decrease | ~No impact |
| **Mechanism** | Higher depreciation in Cost of Revenue | Depreciation (and impairment) added back |
| **Magnitude (1-year change)** | ~$480M annual / ~$170M quarterly | ~$0 |
| **Economic Signal** | Captures accelerated capital consumption | Obscures accelerated capital consumption |

**Bottom line:** Shorter satellite useful lives would hit Operating Income immediately and materially through increased depreciation in Cost of Revenue, while Segment Adjusted EBITDA would remain unaffected because depreciation is a reconciling add-back. This divergence is analytically significant: Adjusted EBITDA would not reflect the increased capital intensity required to sustain the constellation, making Operating Income (or free cash flow) the more informative metric for assessing the economic consequences of satellite life shortening.

---

**Sources:**

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Significant Accounting Policies (PP&E/Satellite Depreciation), MD&A (Connectivity Segment Cost of Revenue drivers, Sensitivity Disclosure), and Segment Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation"
    }
  ]
}

### 1. First-Affected Line Items

If Starlink satellite useful lives proved shorter than assumed, the following line items would be affected **first and most directly**:

| Order | Line Item | Direction | Mechanism |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **1st** | **Depreciation Expense** (within Cost of Revenue) | ↑ Increase | Shorter useful life → higher periodic straight-line depreciation charge per satellite |
| **2nd** | **Cost of Revenue** (Connectivity segment) | ↑ Increase | Satellite depreciation is explicitly classified within the Connectivity segment's Cost of Revenue (inclusive of launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure depreciation) |

A scenario in which satellites last only 3 years instead of 5 would require significantly higher ongoing capital expenditure to maintain constellation density, but this cash outflow would appear only in CapEx (investing activities), not in Adjusted EBITDA.
- The filing itself flags this as a **critical accounting estimate**, noting that management periodically reviews useful life assumptions with engineering teams based on on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions.
- The company also acknowledges that it **frequently deorbits satellites before the end of their estimated useful lives** for safety and collision-avoidance reasons, making this a live risk rather than a hypothetical one.

### 5. Summary

| | Operating Income | Segment Adjusted EBITDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Impact** | ↓ Decrease | ~No impact |
| **Mechanism** | Higher depreciation in Cost of Revenue | Depreciation (and impairment) added back |
| **Magnitude (1-year change)** | ~$480M annual / ~$170M quarterly | ~$0 |
| **Economic Signal** | Captures accelerated capital consumption | Obscures accelerated capital consumption |

**Bottom line:** Shorter satellite useful lives would hit Operating Income immediately and materially through increased depreciation in Cost of Revenue, while Segment Adjusted EBITDA would remain unaffected because depreciation is a reconciling add-back. This divergence is analytically significant: Adjusted EBITDA would not reflect the increased capital intensity required to sustain the constellation, making Operating Income (or free cash flow) the more informative metric for assessing the economic consequences of satellite life shortening.
